title: Mast-o khiar
subtitle: Persian cucumber and yoghurt dip with dried mint and walnuts
excerpt: Thick yoghurt mixed with finely diced cucumber, garlic, dried mint, and crushed walnuts, a Persian dip that is simultaneously cooling and substantial. Served chilled as part of a mezze spread, alongside bread, or as a companion to Persian rice dishes. The dried mint and walnuts are what distinguish it from Greek tzatziki.
sourceNotes: Mast-o khiar (Persian for 'yoghurt and cucumber') is a foundational Persian condiment documented in Margaret Shaida's 'The Legendary Cuisine of Persia' (1992) and Najmieh Batmanglij's 'Food of Life' (1986). It appears on the Iranian table at most meals as a cooling counterpoint to spiced stews and grilled meats. The walnuts and dried mint are the distinctly Persian elements.

content.text: The cucumber must be deseeded and the excess water removed before mixing with the yoghurt. Undrained cucumber turns the dip thin and watery within minutes. Grate or finely dice the cucumber, then sprinkle with a little salt and leave in a colander for 10 minutes. Squeeze in a cloth to remove any remaining water. The yoghurt should be thick, full-fat Greek yoghurt works well; the labneh drained for 24 hours is even better.

The walnuts are crushed roughly, not ground. They should provide texture, not disappear into the dip. A few fresh or dried rose petals on top are traditional and add a faint fragrance. The garlic can be adjusted to taste; a little goes a long way in a cold dip.

items.name: Cucumber
items.prepNote: peeled, deseeded, and finely diced or grated

items.name: Garlic
items.prepNote: crushed to a paste with salt

content.text: Salt the diced or grated {{cucumber}} and leave in a sieve for 10 minutes. Squeeze firmly in a cloth or tea towel to remove all remaining moisture. Combine the {{plain-yoghurt}}, drained cucumber, {{garlic}} paste, and {{sea-salt-fine}} in a bowl. Stir in the {{rosewater}} if using. Fold through the {{walnuts}} if using. Cover and refrigerate for at least 30 minutes. Just before serving, crush the {{mint}} between your fingers and scatter over the top.

items.fix: Drain the cucumber thoroughly, the squeeze in a cloth is important, not optional. The yoghurt itself may also need to be thicker: strain it through a muslin for 30 minutes first
Crush the garlic with a pinch of salt to a smooth paste, which mellows its sharpness. For a more delicate version, use half a clove only
items.cause: Too much garlic or raw garlic not crushed with salt

items.fix: Start with half a teaspoon and taste before adding more. Rosewater is powerful, a little adds fragrance, too much makes the dip taste like soap
Use full-fat yoghurt, or strain any yoghurt through muslin for 1 hour to thicken it. Greek yoghurt is consistently thick and reliable for this
items.symptom: Dip is too loose and doesn't hold on a spoon

content.text: Mast-o khiar is one of the masts that run through Persian cooking. A mast is a yoghurt-based dip or side. Other well-known versions are mast-o moosir, with dried shallots, and mast-e khiar ba gol-e mohammadi, with rose petals. The mix of cucumber and yoghurt shows up across the Middle East and Central Asia in slightly different forms. The Greek tzatziki, the Indian raita, and the Turkish cacik all belong to the same family. The dried mint and walnuts give the Persian version its own character. Served cold alongside spiced rice and grilled meat, it does what every yoghurt side does: cools, refreshes, and balances.
